nativeRange.habitat: Rubble and low patch reefs in areas of current, typically on outer reef slopes
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical reef rubble and patch-reef zones reported at roughly 2–40 m depth

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding on zooplankton in the water column. In aquaria generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty foods, and quality marine flake or pellets. Several small feedings per day tend to suit the species' active mid-water foraging style.

avoidWith: other male fairy wrasses in the same tank

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer Georgina Jones (imported from iNaturalist); 640x428. Sharp in-situ reef photo of a small group of Cirrhilabrus rubripinnis at Puerto Galera, Philippines; central fish shows the diagnostic pink-red body and bright red/magenta dorsal, anal and caudal fins, and multiple individuals suit this haremic species.

summary: A small, brightly colored fairy wrasse from the Philippines and Indonesia, generally considered a peaceful and reef-safe addition to mature medium-to-large reef aquariums.
careNotes: The Redfin Wrasse (Cirrhilabrus rubripinnis) is native to outer-reef rubble zones and low patch reefs across the Western Central Pacific, particularly around the Philippines and Indonesia, where it inhabits depths of roughly 2 to 40 m in areas of moderate current. Generally considered peaceful and reef-safe, with coral and ornamental invertebrate nipping not commonly reported, though some individuals may become more assertive over time. Like most fairy wrasses, the Redfin Wrasse is a notable escape artist and has been reported to bolt straight upward when startled; a tightly fitted lid or eurobrace (a rim of glass or acrylic that closes the gap at the top of an open-top aquarium) is strongly recommended. This species is best kept as a single male, or as a male introduced alongside one or more females to form a small harem; multiple males in the same tank tend to fight. Individuals are protogynous hermaphrodites — meaning they begin life as females and may later transition to male — and males display heightened color intensity with notably enlarged red dorsal and anal fins during courtship displays.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species page used for water parameters, tank size (90 gal / ~340 L), diet, temperament (peaceful), care level (easy), reef-safe rating, and jumping note.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed family Labridae, order Eupercaria, max length 9.1 cm TL, depth range 2–40 m, distribution Philippines/Indonesia, and zooplankton-feeding behavior in small male-led groups.

additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (3.1 in / ~8 cm) and tank size (55 gal / ~208 L). Noted as 'semi aggressive — may become quite aggressive as time goes on,' which biases overall temperament slightly above LiveAquaria's 'peaceful.'
